# AFL great Mick Malthouse struck alleged intruder in home raid, court hears

By court reporter [Kristian Silva](https://www.abc.net.au/news/kristian-silva/7115474)

- [Topic:Courts](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/courts)

18h ago18 hours agoMon 27 Apr 2026 at 5:38am

![Mick Malthouse is pictured at the MCG](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/9cc7b700f150d689c9e412c04c27e7ff?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=975&cropW=1732&xPos=0&yPos=54&width=862&height=485)

AFL legend Mick Malthouse was injured during an alleged attempted home invasion last year.(ABC Grandstand: Damien Peck)

## In short:

AFL great Mick Malthouse fought off intruders with an exercise bar as he defended his family during an alleged attempted home invasion in Melbourne last year, a court has heard.

New details about the alleged break-in attempt were revealed in court today, as one of the three men charged made a bail application.

The trio are yet to enter a plea in respect to the alleged break-in of Malthouse's property.

Legendary AFL coach Mick Malthouse smashed an intruder in the head with an exercise bar as he defended his family during an alleged attempted home invasion last year, a Melbourne court has heard.

New details about the alleged break-in attempt and surrounding events were revealed in court today, as one of the three men charged made a bail application.

Victoria Police detective Tyler Sherlock alleged one of the accused, Joshua Di Cello, stole a key fob from a construction company that completed cladding work on Mr Malthouse's apartment complex on September 1 last year.

Mr Di Cello and co-accused Aysar Mashu and Andrew Carroll allegedly arrived at the complex just after midnight on September 4, and used an internal stairwell to try to break into Mr Malthouse's penthouse, Mr Sherlock said.

"Malthouse described hearing noises from the front door … Malthouse confronted Di Cello, Mashu and Carroll and observed them in possession of a jimmy bar, actively trying to break through the door," he told the court.

"Malthouse swung an exercise pole at them before they retreated to the basement."

![Mick Malthouse adjusts his glasses at the football](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/a7a44a315f4a257a9aa3f4e72f69a5af?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1880&cropW=2817&xPos=1&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Mick Malthouse coached 718 AFL games.(AAP: Julian Smith)

A second confrontation occurred when Mr Malthouse, 72, followed the group, who were by that stage allegedly trying to break open a door to flee, the court heard.

"Carroll raised a jimmy bar he was in possession of. Malthouse struck Carroll in the head with the exercise bar causing Carroll to drop it," Mr Sherlock said.

Mr Malthouse was stabbed in the chest with a screwdriver by Mr Mashu, the court heard. The injuries were superficial and did not cause any organ damage.

About an hour later, Mr Carroll was seen on CCTV with "a significant welt under his left eye", Mr Sherlock said.

The court heard Mr Malthouse later told officers, "I managed to crack one of them with the exercise bar … I whacked him twice."

## Other properties allegedly targeted

Police allege Mr Di Cello worked with others to commit a series of property and car thefts between August and September 2025.

![A man makes a peace sign with his hands in a selfie photo.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e48c340ac5cebf811d1e9a0af215bdce?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=382&width=862&height=575)

It is alleged Joshua Di Cello and two others attempted to break in to Mick Malthouse's apartment last year.(Supplied)

Locations targeted included jewellery shops, offices and a Dandenong South property where $60,000 worth of boxed children's toys were stolen, Mr Sherlock said.

He is alleged to have been decked out in Nike sportswear during several of the incidents.

Mr Di Cello, Mr Mashu and Mr Carroll have since been arrested and are on remand.

The trio are yet to enter a plea in respect to the alleged break-in of Mr Malthouse's property.

Mr Di Cello's lawyer, Nadia Giogianni, said her client was seeking bail so he could attend a drug rehabilitation centre. She also said her client, 36, was a vulnerable person with an intellectual disability.

However, Mr Sherlock said it was in the community's interests for him to remain in custody, citing his lengthy criminal history.

"He's a persistent, prolific thief," Mr Sherlock said.

A magistrate denied Mr Di Cello's bail application this afternoon.

# Australian Gregory Louden on leading PlayStation's next big title, Saros

- [![](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/418fc9df7d35636a475e0a6f0b4cdde2?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1120&cropW=1120&xPos=720&yPos=0&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
G\\
\\
By Gianfranco Di Giovanni](https://www.abc.net.au/news/gianfranco-di-giovanni/10982218)
- [ABC Arts](http://www.abc.net.au/arts/)
- [Topic:Video Games](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/video-games)

22h ago22 hours agoSun 26 Apr 2026 at 10:00pm

If you look at the work of game developer Gregory Louden you'll notice a pretty constant running theme: our protagonist takes time to walk the same path again and again, a little faster, a little more confidently each time.

So it's no surprise that his latest and biggest title Saros, out this week, continues to build on that theme that using everything you've experienced before is the path forward.

Louden always wanted to be a game developer, but getting to that very first game credit took a sideways career step and a persistence to keep pushing onwards.

Born and raised in Sydney, Louden started his career in visual effects working on films like Sucker Punch, Gravity and Prometheus, which led him to Europe: first to London then Helsinki, where he still lives.

"So professionally, I always loved games, but just couldn't find my foot in the door," he tells ABC Arts.

"I managed to work on a lot of great movies, but my passion for games didn't go away."

![A man with a beard holds a poster and looks towards the camera, poster has a koala on it.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/7ca6924454b75a607c6b4ea4e5fa782c?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2016&cropW=3024&xPos=0&yPos=583&width=862&height=575)

Gregory Louden also developed independent video game Stone, a neo-noir adventure set in Australia. (Supplied: Gregory Louden, Housemarque / PlayStation Australia)

After securing a job at Finnish studio Remedy Games, the first title Louden shipped was Quantum Break, a time-looping adventure that included a dynamic interstitial filmed television show that changed depending on player actions.

Moving studios to Housemarque, also based in Finland, Louden became the narrative director on Returnal, another science fiction adventure about a lost astronaut caught in a time loop.

Even his independent work on Stone, an Australian neo-noir adventure where you play as a koala with a hangover, has players retracing the titular character's steps to discover their missing love.

Louden says while it wasn't a deliberate decision, it's unsurprising that others have identified this recurring theme in his work.

"I have noticed it and while it's unintentional, I think it comes from a place where a good story is often a loop; it's about setting something up, and coming back to it with growth and change," Louden says.

"We live in time, so studying characters' choices and then what they can change has always been interesting to me."

## Again and again

While players in Saros will revisit the same levels as they unravel the mystery of the alien world of Carcosa, Louden says it is important to keep adjusting and changing the road in front of them.

![A screenshot of the video game Saros showing many projectiles being shot towards the player. ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/fe58657ce8e972ac3b8f54c11bc55546?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2160&cropW=3240&xPos=300&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Players need to memorise boss patterns in order to complete levels, but failing and dying is a part of the game's progression system. (Supplied: Housemarque / PlayStation Studios)

One particular run might introduce a new narrative location or even change the enemies players need to overcome.

"Our world needs to surprise you; this game shapeshifts, it's not like other games where you keep playing through the same path — every experience you go through it changes," Louden says.

"Our music and sound design is constantly changing and evolving our gameplay; we're rotating out the things that you can collect.

> "So it's about this sense of layering the gameplay and the experience for you, so it can survive repetition. You're supposed to die in Saros. That's the point. It's about coming back stronger."

## Authentic characterisation

Actor Rahul Kohli plays Arjun Devraj, a soldier for the mysterious Soltari corporation, and is the character players control throughout Saros.

Louden says it was important Kohli be able to bring real experience to the role.

"The first discussion with Rahul was that 'I want you to be Rahul, you're from London, let's do London'," Louden says.

"I was wanting to push the medium and challenge it. You don't usually hear, like, a London accent like that."

Supporting character Jerome Jackson, played by Australian actor Ben Prendergast, also uses Australian slang.

Louden didn't want it to be just a caricature or stereotypical portrayal.

"The same way you don't usually hear like a Melburnian accent in a game, working with Ben, we did add some Australian slang and we chose in the script where we'd use that," he says.

"Authenticity was the goal, Australian or not: it was just about working with Ben to allow him to really create a special character for players."

Authentic portrayals of Australians in games has long been a passion for Louden.

![A cartoon koala stands in messy office, a video game screenshot ](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/45eaf641ff675ebf392b96a12ae0ac59?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1620&xPos=150&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

The very Australian game Stone is a single-player, third-person interactive story, where a hungover koala detective wakes to find his lover has been kidnapped. (Supplied: Convict Games)

The 2018 game Stone features anthropomorphic Australian animals, and players can visit locations like a suburban pub or a bowls club as they search for their lost partner, with Louden's friends and family providing voices for the characters.

## A dream project

Louden says Saros, which he considers his dream project, represents an important milestone and has taken a massive team to make it happen.

"When I get to think of all these different aspects and collaborators \[on the game\], it's been amazing, and it's really a dream team," he says.

"I've always dreamed of wanting to work on a big project like Saros, and I feel like I've got so much more to learn."

Louden says there is something magic about all the disciplines that are required to make a game come together to make something unique.

"I love music, I love film, I love television, I love games, but the reason why I work in games is that it's a combination of all of them where you can take everything you can learn from music, from film, from television, and you can apply the most interesting kind of multiplier, which is interaction," Louden says.

"It's been such a huge team effort that I'm really proud and humbled to get to represent everyone that contributed so much of themselves into this game."

**Saros is out on April 30.**

# Saudi Arabia's dazzling vision was crashing into reality even before the Iran war broke out

By Middle East correspondent [Matthew Doran](https://www.abc.net.au/news/matthew-doran/5511636) in Riyadh

- [Foreign Correspondent](https://www.abc.net.au/news/programs/foreign/)
- [Topic:World Politics](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/world-politics)

12m ago12 minutes agoMon 27 Apr 2026 at 3:02pm

![An image of the proposed The Line ending at the sea](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/5a158f2e3899d818cf5eae1e6249a368?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1080&cropW=1920&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=485)

An image of the proposed The Line development in Saudi Arabia. (Supplied: NEOM)

The war in the Middle East is forcing one of the region's biggest players to rethink some of its grand plans for the future, with Saudi Crown Prince Mohammed bin Salman's trillion-dollar Vision 2030 rocked by the conflict.

The blueprint for Saudi Arabia's future, unveiled a decade ago, had outlined a glittering future for the kingdom as a hub for major events and sport to compete with its Gulf neighbours, with futuristic cities rising out of the desert sands.

Even before the US and Israel launched strikes against Iran at the end of February, Vision 2030 was under strain, with speculation some projects could be scrapped or dramatically scaled back as the plan's staggering ambition crashed into financial reality.

Now, amid unprecedented retaliatory attacks by Iran on its Gulf neighbours including Saudi Arabia and massive disruption to global shipping routes due to the effective closure of the Strait of Hormuz, it's facing the perfect storm.

![A middle-aged man looks seriously at the camera.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/76af3c8c29643026bdc4eb3a430e281f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2553&cropW=3830&xPos=0&yPos=2&width=862&height=575)

Neil Quilliam says the war in Iran has struck a blow to Saudi's grand ambitions. (Foreign Correspondent: Hamish Harty)

According to Dr Neil Quilliam, an associate fellow at Chatham House who specialises in global energy policy and Middle East politics, the war is directly affecting Saudi's ability to drum up funding.

"The vision is very much based on attracting foreign direct investment, so getting foreign companies to come and to commit to the country long-term. If there's instability in the region, then that's a great deterrence for international investors to come into the country," Dr Quilliam told Foreign Correspondent.

"Given that the country, number one, wants to attract and develop its tourist space, and number two, wants to be the host of international and regional events, instability is a drag on that."

## Breaking Saudi's oil dependence

Vision 2030 is designed to help Saudi Arabia shift away from its dependence on oil to fill government coffers by transforming the country into an international investment, technology, tourism, sport and cultural powerhouse.

The Crown Prince, known as MBS, has his fingerprints all over the plan, as he seeks to reshape the country he will one day rule as king and prepare it to host one of the world's largest sporting festivals, the 2034 FIFA World Cup.

Much of the work is being steered by Saudi Arabia's sovereign wealth vehicle, the Public Investment Fund (PIF), which itself is worth more than $1.5 trillion.

![A man smiles.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/dad5fddc7870b6cce20c94c064887b54?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Mohammed bin Salman at the US–Saudi Investment Forum in Washington DC last year. (Reuters: Evelyn Hockstein)

In late 2025, Saudi Arabia's own budget reports showed more than half of the country's annual revenue came from the oil industry.

Since the Iran war broke out, Saudi Arabia's oil infrastructure has come under Iranian attack, including the critical East–West oil pipeline, which enabled them to bypass Iran's chokehold on the Strait of Hormuz by piping oil west to ports on the Red Sea.

It's since been repaired, but Saudi has not been able to restore exports to pre-war volumes through its usual shipping lanes.

While surging oil prices are providing some relief to the budget bottom line, the supply chain interruption has only served to bolster MBS's thesis that the country needs to scale back its reliance on oil revenue.

![Tourists in the desert.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/af07e2b1160964e064b9e54f3a7a16c0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Saudi Arabia has been ramping up its tourism industry to try to reduce economic dependence on oil revenue. (Foreign Correspondent: Matt Davis)

But doing so has been more complicated than the Crown Prince initially bargained for.

## Giga-plan visions in the desert

Some of Vision 2030's big-ticket items, often referred to as giga-projects, are massive city developments rising from the sands of the Saudi desert.

Perhaps the most ambitious and well-known example is NEOM, a region in the country's north-west, which was to include everything from luxury island resorts to a ski village.

Seen as MBS's signature project, its centrepiece was The Line, a surreal 170km-long linear city supposed to house 9 million people.

With an eye-watering reported initial price tag of $2.2 trillion, and with $80 billion already poured into NEOM, The Line has reportedly been wound back to a far more modest proposition.

![mirror-line-saudi-arabia-linear-city-neom-4](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/c6c35cf99fba6a21b4cedb0072858a8e?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=533&cropW=800&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

An artist's impression of part of The Line from above. (Supplied: NEOM)

Earlier this month, the governor of the PIF, Yasir Al-Rymayyan, insisted NEOM remained a key project.

"It is a major global economic zone," he said. "No project in NEOM has been cancelled. Has anything been postponed? Certainly, yes.

"Why? Some projects have been delayed because they are not on the critical path. There is no critical impact."

But despite that, the PIF has announced spending as part of Vision 2030 was being cut back or reprioritised.

One area likely to be affected is the breakaway LIV Golf competition.

"The economy was already beginning to contract, young Saudis were always already beginning to sort of be laid off in the economy, so there had to be a recalibration, a refocus," said Dr Quilliam.

![An archway entrance with LIV Golf written above it.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/0d77470ad962c9d8fa1e69806d9a16c7?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2688&cropW=4032&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Funding for the LIV Golf breakaway tournament is among the Saudi initiatives now facing an uncertain future. (Foreign Correspondent: Matthew Doran)

"And then, obviously with the conflict, that's really kind of pushed the moment that they need to unveil the strategy — and it's all about showing business as usual."

## Putting Saudi 'at the axis' of global sport

Before the war, Foreign Correspondent travelled to Saudi Arabia to witness the dramatic transformation taking shape.

One example of the country's jostling for global attention could be seen at a provincial football stadium in the city of Buraidah, 350km north-west of the capital, Riyadh.

More than 10,000 fans were waiting to watch a Friday night match between Saudi Pro league minnows Al Kholood and one of the competition's so-called "big four", Al Nassr, captained by football superstar Cristiano Ronaldo.

![A player on the football field.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/f9a7296fcc7464e1039839878638ec25?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2546&cropW=3819&xPos=25&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Cristiano Ronaldo takes the field for Saudi club Al Nassr in February this year. (Foreign Correspondent: Matthew Doran)

![A group of young people in stadium seating cheer for the camera.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/4db2a18ecb62115dce85132476f8fe3d?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Young football fans at a match between Saudi clubs Al Kholood and Al Nassr. (Foreign Correspondent: Matt Davis)

While he had spent most of his career playing in front of far larger crowds, the 40-year-old's tenure in the kingdom has cemented his status as the world's highest-paid athlete.

His last contract extension was reportedly worth more than $345 million per season and was said to include an equity stake in Al Nassr.

"I think people in the region, maybe, were following the clubs and the competition and some of the big players, but it wasn't at the tip of anyone's tongue, let's say, outside of the region," said Al Kholood's owner, Ben Harburg.

The American investor, originally from New Mexico, said that was now changing.

![A man in the stands at a football game.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/16f9fcc901ae08b54a93cf98d4e16c88?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Ben Harburg (centre) says Saudi Arabia is becoming a global centre for sport. (Foreign Correspondent: Matt Davis)

He was the first foreigner to own a Saudi football team after being invited by the PIF to buy Al Kholood and signing the deal for an undisclosed sum.

"I repeat this over and over, ad nauseam, but I don't think that football is a great investment, I think there are much easier, stress-free ways to make money," he said.

"But the reality is that the poles of global sport have shifted, and Saudi Arabia now very much sits at the axis of a lot of the things that are going on."

Expats from around the world are among those Vision 2030 is relying on to help guide the transformation.

Danny Townsend, the former chief of Australian football's A-Leagues, is now the chief executive of Saudi Arabia's SURJ Sports, the kingdom's sport-focused investment vehicle.

![A man behind a desk.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/19b1a3c7ba7757c5049c3d883bae3532?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Danny Townsend is among the expats taking part in Saudi Arabia's transformation. (Foreign Correspondent: Matt Davis)

"When you think about where the country's come from in the last 15 years, certainly we were a lot further behind a lot of our other Gulf neighbours," he said.

"And I think we've made a conscious effort to try to accelerate that growth in order to meet the market demand that is here.

"I think there's an impatience as well. The crown prince certainly has got a bold vision and he wants to achieve what he wants to achieve as quickly as he can."

## Accusations of 'sportswashing'

The scale of the transformation in Saudi Arabia has its critics, with some accusing MBS of "sportswashing": using sports to distract from the country's record on human rights.

British journalist and author James Montague, who wrote the book Engulfed, examining the role of sport in the new Saudi Arabia, believes the term is too simplistic.

![A man at his computer.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/e8cf06e95bee7979462d61c1def8048a?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2827&cropW=4240&xPos=0&yPos=3&width=862&height=575)

James Montague says the world has gotten over its qualms about the 2018 murder of journalist Jamal Khashoggi. (Foreign Correspondent)

"What's going on here is something much more powerful, it's much more political, it's soft power," said Montague.

"It's using sport in a way that's not only to distract, it's to rebuild, and it's also about making yourself economically and culturally indispensable to the rest of the world."

Vision 2030 relies on more than just Saudi oil money, with the country seeking around $140 billion in foreign investment as part of the plans.

The 2018 murder of Saudi journalist Jamal Khashoggi in the country's embassy in Istanbul, which US intelligence said was ordered by MBS, spooked some investors.

Richard Branson was one of those who pulled out of deals in the kingdom in protest.

![headshot of Jamal Khashoggi wearing a white head covering and glasses, mid-speech.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/2a04589cd1f412b562ec5b373ec372c7?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1997&cropW=3000&xPos=0&yPos=17&width=862&height=575)

US intelligence agencies concluded Saudi journalist Jamal Khashoggi's murder was approved by MBS. (AP: Hasan Jamali, file)

"Ultimately, the murder of Jamal Khashoggi, as close as it came to ruining MBS's reputation — the world moved past it," said Montague.

"What I think MBS, and certainly his cadre of followers and people around him worked out, is that ultimately the West, they follow the money and they came back.

"It's almost difficult to imagine any single act that Saudi Arabia could do that could stop people wanting to make business with it, especially when you have Trump in the White House."

## The 'worst-case scenario' for Saudi's grand vision

That theory is likely to be tested if hostilities between the US and Iran drag on and continue to involve the wider Gulf region.

Michael Ratney, the Biden administration's Ambassador to Saudi Arabia, believes the current war and the uncertainty about the future is the "worst-case scenario" for Saudi.

![An aircraft hit by a missile.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/a8459a13a5c53949dbe4aeeacc32c6e0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1024&cropW=1536&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

A US aircraft lies damaged on the runway in a strike at the Prince Sultan Air Base in Saudi Arabia during the Iran war in March. (Reuters)

"This is sort of the mother of all conflicts in the region — this is their major antagonist and adversary in a full-on war with the United States, and the Saudis and the Emiratis and everyone else in the Gulf is right in the middle of it," he said.

Mr Ratney, who has not been replaced in Riyadh by the Trump White House, has argued the Saudis have "no love for the Iranian regime."

"They would love to see them dispatched. On the other hand, I think they're pretty sober about the ability of the US or Israel to do that," he said.

"And I think they're worried about being stuck with the results of an inconclusive war, especially if Iran retains control of the Strait of Hormuz."

![A construction site in the desert.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/ad0470e722c8210953bd04e1ff69cb77?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3173&cropW=4760&xPos=31&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Construction underway at Quiddiyah, a planned city outside Riyadh. (Foreign Correspondent: Matthew Doran)

![A roller coaster in the desert.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/f17e6c2465ad1529cb6736769d24d3ff?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3264&cropW=4896&xPos=26&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

A roller-coaster built by American theme park giant Six Flags at Quiddiya reaches speeds of 250km/h. (Foreign Correspondent: Matthew Doran)

Saudi Arabia host US forces and the country's embassy in Riyadh has been targeted by a drone attack.

There has been debate as to whether the war, and specifically Iran's attacks, would prompt some regional allies to reconsider their ties with the United States.

"That's unknowable until the dust settles, until the war is over and they and we and others in the region have had an opportunity to think this through," said Ratney.

"It's easy to say, 'Oh, they're disappointed in the United States, they're going to look for partnerships elsewhere.'

"On the other hand, they have a lot invested in the relationship with the US.

"It may well be that they decide that the US is going to continue to be their partner, they would like greater clarity about the relationship, but meanwhile, they hedge and they pursue deeper partnerships with others at the same time."

_**Watch Saudi's Reality Check tonight on Foreign Correspondent at 8pm on ABC TV and ABC iview.**_

# Orthopaedic surgeon Herwig Drobetz returns to Gaza for fourth mercy mission

By [Hannah Ross](https://www.abc.net.au/news/hannah-ross/10963084) and [Joanne Shoebridge](https://www.abc.net.au/news/joanne-shoebridge/7095396)

- [ABC North Coast](https://www.abc.net.au/northcoast/)
- [Topic:War Victims](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/war-victims)

18h ago18 hours agoSun 26 Apr 2026 at 10:10pm

![Herwig looks serious, has grey stubble, wears glasses, beanie, scrubs, looks at camera, medical equipment behind.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/27c17f360da9f3834f8d1ed09571a128?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=675&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=463&width=862&height=485)

Herwig Drobetz has returned to Gaza over the weekend. (Supplied: Herwig Drobetz)

## In short:

An Australian orthopaedic surgeon is returning to Gaza for his fourth mission to work at the Rafah Field Hospital.

Herwig Drobetz says people are in dire need of post-operative care and medical supplies.

## What's next?

A new report by the European Union and the United Nations has found more than $US71 billion ($99.4 billion) will be needed over the next 10 years for recovery and reconstruction in Gaza

Orthopaedic surgeon Herwig Drobetz has felt a range of emotions following his 2024 stint as a Red Cross medic in Gaza.

Relief to be back home doing his job as director of the trauma unit at Lismore Base Hospital in northern NSW.

Guilt for leaving behind his colleagues and the many thousands of people needing medical care in the war-torn enclave.

![Field hospital with sun setting, tents in a row, vehicle with red cross on top.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/fe87ad35964e599d9f50fe34e1fca02f?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=586&width=862&height=575)

Herwig Drobetz says the work at the Rafa Field Hospital is demanding. (Supplied: Herwig Drobetz)

And the anxiety after weeks of living and working with minimal sleep and adrenaline coursing through his veins.

"\[2024\] was the first time that I was in an active war zone and there was bombs flying and bombs getting dropped and the tank shelling and shelling from the ocean over the hospital," Dr Drobetz said.

"The hospital coordinates were blanked out, the GPS coordinates, so they couldn't actively drop bombs from drones or from planes onto that humanitarian zone.

> "But if they drop a bomb a kilometre away, it still makes the whole building shake and the ceiling tiles fall off."

![Doctor in scrubs heading into a Gaza hospital emergency department.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/dbd488a9fc355e6771fd55826370f2f8?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=601&width=862&height=575)

Herwig Drobetz says he had never been in an active war zone before. (Supplied: Herwig Drobetz)

Dr Drobetz said perhaps the most horrible thing was the way he got used to it.

"The first time this happens, you are lying under your bed and it's really scary because you also feel the pressure wave," he said.

"Sometimes we had gunfights breaking out like 50 metres away from us and we are operating in a tent.

> "You don't notice how much stress you are under the whole time."

![29 nurses and doctors in scrubs, male and female standing, facing the camera in front of tents on sandy soil](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/20e8fae9500af4b924c1529d3b7a837c?src)

A Red Cross official says doctors work every day and are exposed to horrific human trauma. (Supplied: Herwig Drobetz)

Despite this, Dr Drobetz is returning to Gaza this week for his fourth mission, where he will be based at Rafah Field Hospital, working in a paid capacity for the International Committee of the Red Cross (ICRC).

ICRC spokesperson Pat Griffiths said 14 international medical staff were currently working at the Red Cross Field Hospital in Rafah in southern Gaza, side-by-side with Palestinian medics.

Mr Griffiths said the doctors worked every day and were exposed to horrific human trauma.

"It's intense work and it never really stops, so they are on quite short rotations, but many of them do come back several times," he said.

> "It's hard work, but very important work and we are very glad when colleagues who have a bit of experience are able to return."

## Need still dire

Mr Griffiths said the medical system in Gaza was at breaking point.

"There has been more than two years of fighting here and that just builds pressure because the medical need of the population grows," he said.

> "Meanwhile, the medical system itself has become a casualty of the conflict."

According to the final Gaza Rapid Damage and Needs Assessment (RDNA), 60 per cent of Gaza's population is homeless after 24 months of conflict.

The new assessment by the European Union (EU) and the United Nations (UN), jointly conducted with the World Bank, estimated $US71.4 billion ($99.4 billion) would be needed for reconstruction over the next decade.

Health is among the hardest-hit sectors, along with housing, education and agriculture.

![A crowded hospital ward with people walking.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/abe19bae7ebe2dabd76e8e9d97a1546e?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=400&width=862&height=575)

Herwig Drobetz says people live in the hospital grounds and every available space inside the hospital (Supplied: Herwig Drobetz)

The United Nations Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs said violence [had also persisted in Gaza despite the ceasefire](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-16/palestinians-fear-gaza-is-being-forgotten-as-iran-war-drags-on/106565594) coming into effect in October last year.

Dr Drobetz said, although the "circus" of international attention had now shifted to other parts of the Middle East, the people of Gaza were still in dire need of help.

He said hospitals were filled with patients requiring complex care.

"There are people with amputated limbs who have infections, who can't walk because they have wound problems," Dr Drobetz said.

> "This is actually the much more difficult issue now because there's thousands of people who need highly complicated and complex operations."

![A white board says ICRC field hopsital, white Tents line inside.](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/d2eaa006f9c1aa30f3fdd5f6a2a4f082?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=800&cropW=1200&xPos=0&yPos=400&width=862&height=575)

Herwig Drobetz says International Red Cross is transitioning Rafa Field Hospital to a permanent structure. (Supplied: Herwig Drobetz)

## Eager to return

Dr Drobetz said ever since he was a junior doctor, he knew he wanted to work in conflict zones.

Despite the horrors of human suffering he witnessed in Gaza, Dr Drobetz was eager to return.

"There's no X-ray, there's no MRI, there's no CT scan, there's no implants, all these things, which is medically challenging," he said.

> "That's something I'm interested in, you have to do many things with nothing."

Dr Drobetz said part of him was also drawn to the adventure.

"I think that everybody who does these things feels the same way."

# Sabastian Sawe ran the marathon in an 'unbelievable time'. Here's why we can believe in it

- [![Simon Smale](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/f87ce62e8c54de2bd5b3ed71ee8e48b0?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=893&cropW=893&xPos=103&yPos=16&width=160&height=160)\\
\\
S\\
\\
By Simon Smale](https://www.abc.net.au/news/simon-smale/10007892)
- [Topic:Marathon](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/marathon)

18h ago18 hours agoSun 26 Apr 2026 at 8:37pm

![Sabastian Sawe stands next to his record time](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/190aecbb1673b89d7249aa47636b4e77?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2370&cropW=4213&xPos=533&yPos=120&width=862&height=485)

Sabastian Sawe will go down in history as the first man to ever break the two-hour barrier in an official race. (Getty Images: WireImage/Karwai Tang)

When Sabastian Sawe broke the tape on The Mall on Sunday, having run the London Marathon in a stunning 1 hour 59 minutes and 30 seconds, Steve Cram was speaking for so many gob-smacked running fans.

"That, you would say, is unbelievable, but we have just seen it," the former track world champion said on the BBC.

"Nobody has ever done this. They said it couldn't be done."

Any time the word "unbelievable" is used in a sporting context, an alarm bell normally starts ringing.

Even those who resolutely channel the X-Files' Agent Fox Mulder in their desperation to believe in the impossible sometimes need a degree of level-headed Sherlock Holmesian pessimism as the only rational discourse — accepting the most likely explanation, however unsavoury, as the real truth.

![Sabastian Sawe holds out his hands](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/5c772051fdd0b303f24fb1b8cdfdb03a?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1877&cropW=2815&xPos=315&yPos=787&width=862&height=575)

Kenyan distance running has a new king in Sabastian Sawe. (Getty Images: Alex Davidson)

Athletics is not alone in being plagued by doping scandals — performances are questioned time and time again in recent years across a multitude of sports — but it has had a particularly bad run of late.

Women's world record holder, [Ruth Chepngetich, is serving a three-year ban for testing positive for the banned diuretic hydrochlorothiazide](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-07-18/ruth-chepngetich-marathon-world-record-holder-suspended-doping/105545354).

Chepngetich, rather predictably, denied any wrongdoing, somewhat inexplicably saying she had taken some of her housemaid's medication after feeling hot and having a rapid resting heartbeat.

Athletics Integrity Unit investigators, having studied her text messages and determined that the doping was likely intentional, found her baffling claims to be "hardly credible".

The Kenyan had stunned the world when she ran the Chicago Marathon in 2 hours, 9 minutes, and 56 seconds, almost 2 minutes faster than the previous world record, prompting many to call it "unbelievable".

![Ruth Chepngetich raises her arms](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/beeebf9f7f6d628b7409621336c527d9?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=1729&cropW=2593&xPos=69&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Ruth Chepngetich has been disgraced after testing positive for a banned diuretic. (Getty Images: Chicago Tribune/Tribune News Service/Tess Crowley)

That record still stands — albeit with a suitably-sized asterisk next to it — because the positive test came some time after the run.

In fact, it was days before she was due to run in the 2025 London Marathon that Chepngetich's cheating was exposed when she was a late withdrawal.

That's not the only bombshell cheating claim to come to light in recent months.

Paris Olympian Jackline Sakilu was banned for a massive 10 years after evidence the Tanzanian athlete had microdosed androsterone and etiocholanolone over long periods of time, including during the Paris Games.

Meanwhile, former men's world record holder Wilson Kipsang was banned for four years in 2020 due to missed doping tests and tampering with an anti-doping investigation.

So, when anyone describes a performance as "unbelievable", it's perhaps worth having a closer look.

Fortunately for those who hope to believe what they saw on the streets of London on Sunday, Sawe is one of those who has been asking for a closer look too.

Sawe has spoken out in the past about problems with doping in the sport of marathon running.

And, rather than just cast out phrases of disappointment, he has acted on it, too.

Given Kenya's woeful recent record on doping, the 30-year-old took the extraordinary step of calling for himself to be tested more regularly, inviting the Athletics Integrity Unit (AIU) to test him as often as possible.

![Sabastian Sawe runs with Yomif Kejelcha in a marathon event, flanked by motorcycles](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/6bb3aa25d6b8d3073a4fcd29ad6eb1ee?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2825&cropW=4238&xPos=0&yPos=83&width=862&height=575)

Both Sabastian Sawe and Yomif Kejelcha beat the two-hour mark. (Getty Images: Warren Little)

Last year, it did so, 25 times in fact, in the two months leading up to his attempt at the record in the 2025 Berlin Marathon — an attempt he was on track for before the hot conditions caused him to drop back in pace and settle for the win.

This is reportedly funded by his principle sponsor, adidas, to the tune of tens of thousands of dollars — $50,000 according to his coach, Claudio Berardelli.

"The main reason was to show that I am clean, and I am doing it the right way," Sawe said at the time.

Italian coach Berardelli has had anti doping issues himself in the past, being linked to several camps in Ethiopia where doping was found to be taking place.

Now, he appears to be going to great lengths to ensure his athletes are clean, telling journalists after the race that although the testing had reduced in frequency since the Berlin build-up, Sawe was still under "special anti-doping protocol".

"Yesterday I was with the AIU and they said he had been tested more \[than other athletes\]," he said.

"Of course, what happened was the budget we had before Berlin, the AIU decided to spread it throughout the year because they said it didn't make sense to have him tested every three days, it's too much.

"Still, Sabastian is in a special anti-doping protocol."

The record will be subject to the usual ratification procedures before it is confirmed by World Athletics, and given the unpleasant track record of some athletes, there's no telling if this astonishing run was completely legitimate.

But British Olympic champion Mo Farah seems convinced.

"We've waited long enough to see a human go sub-two," he said, perhaps alluding to the [robot runners of China that recently broke the human half marathon world record](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-20/chinese-humanoid-robot-beats-human-half-marathon-world-record/106585368).

"That's always been the question that we've asked. We've just witnessed something incredible."

## How fast did Sawe run?

![Sabastian Sawe holds up a shoe in his running gear](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/1087d67fb3632c216d3ccc1c1f62d399?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=55&width=862&height=575)

Sabastian Sawe with the now-traditional Monty Python-esque holding up of the shoe. (Getty Images: Karwai Tang)

A milestone-shattering record of this magnitude needs to be put into its appropriate context.

First up, some history.

The two-hour mark has been seen as the holy grail of marathon running, the limit of human capability in much the same way that the four-minute mile was in the immediate post-World War II era.

It was such a pivotal mark that [INEOS was reported to have spent upwards of $20 million on its INEOS 1:59 Challenge in 2019](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2019-10-12/eliud-kipchoge-runs-marathon-in-under-two-hours/11597186).

There, double Olympic champion Eliud Kipchoge did beat the two-hour mark in a run conducted under extreme time-trial conditions, with a laser-guided tracker and rotating pace makers, meaning World Athletics was never going to ratify the time.

But this was in a genuine race — arguably the biggest of them all — the London Marathon.

The time Sawe ran was more than a minute faster than the [late Kelvin Kiptum](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2024-02-12/world-marathon-record-holder-kelvin-kiptum-dead/103454892)'s [previous world record of 2:00:35](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-10-09/kelvin-kiptum-breaks-marathon-world-record-in-chicago/102950382), set in 2023.

Incidentally, Kiptum also held the London Marathon course record of 2:01:25.

![Sabastian Sawe runs past Big Ben in London](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/a99f2356eba394e90f5f3a8477b99ff4?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2000&cropW=3000&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Sabastian Sawe declared himself sporting royalty on the streets of London. (Getty Images: Anadolu/Marcin Nowak)

Earlier in the week, Sawe had told the BBC he was keen to go fast and that it was "only a matter of time" before he broke Kiptum's world record.

He added, "I hope and wish one day \[it will be me\]" to be the one to break the two-hour barrier.

He wasn't the only person to do so on Sunday — the man who finished behind him, marathon debutant Yomif Kejelcha, also broke the near-mythical record, finishing runner-up in 1:59:41, both men pushing each other to greater heights.

That says it all about how good the conditions were — light winds and temperatures in the mid-teens are ideal for marathon running.

Earlier in the day, Tigst Assefa of Ethiopia broke her own women's-only world record en route to victory, crossing the finish line in 2:15.41, beating the record of 2:15.50 she set last year in London.

This is slower than Chepngetich's all-time record because faster elite men can act as pacemakers for women when the races start at the same time.

![Tigst Assefa with her shoe](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/dfee7ff583957f4881b3345d80da802b?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3333&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=168&width=862&height=575)

Tigst Assefa celebrated her own world record with some shoe doodling too. (Getty Images: WireImage/Karwai Tang)

London's flat and fast course has just 75m of elevation gain over 42.2km, similar to Berlin (73m) and Chicago (74m), perennial record-chasing hot spots among marathon majors. Add a generational talent and the latest of [adidas' super shoes,](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2023-09-26/adidas-super-shoe-takes-technological-arms-race-to-new-level/102900712) and you have a recipe for sub-two-hour success.

But even so, this was remarkable.

"There are things that happen in sport, and you want to be there to see history being made," Cram said.

"Roger Bannister \[running\] the first ever four-minute mile — those who were there on that day still tell that story today.

"We said it was a day for records, but I don't think in our wildest dreams we could have foreseen this."

What does that mean for your average punter?

![Sabastian Sawe and Tigst Assefa smile](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/a7aa99b3a633411632d1fa043893848a?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=3084&cropW=4626&xPos=0&yPos=0&width=862&height=575)

Sabastian Sawe and Tigst Assefa broke the men's and women's world record. (Getty Images: Alex Davidson)

Watching a marathon on TV can be desperately misleading — men and women running alongside each other give barely any reference point to show how fast they are actually going.

Standing on course, catching a glimpse of these wisp-like runners blur through a gap between the crowds shows what TV cannot — an insane level of continuous speed that's hard to immediately fathom.

Imagine going down to your local parkrun — which is 5km — and reeling off a time of 14 minutes and 10 seconds. For the record, the average time for an Aussie to complete a parkrun is 33:31.

That's what time Sawe averaged for each 5km of his marathon. However, he did that pace for eight parkruns in a row without a break, then ran another half-distance parkrun for good measure.

Incredibly, he actually ran the second half of his run faster than his first, a phenomenon known as negative splitting.

He went through the first 21 kilometres in 60:29 but put the hammer down for the second half marathon to record a time of just 59:01 — incidentally, only 63 men in history have run a half marathon on its own as quickly as that.

His last 5km block before the finale, from 35km to 40km, was run in 13:42.

Again, for comparison's sake, the fastest ever 5km parkrun stands at 13:44, set by Irish teenager Nick Griggs at the Belfast Victoria parkrun on November 9. The fastest in Australia is James Hansen's 13:53.

"This will reverberate around the world," Paula Radcliffe, a former record holder for the women's marathon, told BBC Sport.

"The goalposts have literally just moved for marathon running and where you benchmark yourself as being world class.

"It is a lesson to everybody out there. We say, 'Don't go out too fast' — they went out smartly and paced it really well."

# New York mayor announces free FIFA World Cup fan zones as costs continue to escalate

- [Topic:FIFA World Cup](https://www.abc.net.au/news/topic/fifa-world-cup)

3h ago3 hours agoMon 27 Apr 2026 at 4:29pm

![Zohran Mamdani speaks. He's wearing a suit and tie](https://live-production.wcms.abc-cdn.net.au/a9868d67a67cac253f949d1e3d1e6174?impolicy=wcms_crop_resize&cropH=2813&cropW=5000&xPos=0&yPos=271&width=862&height=485)

New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani has criticised World Cup ticket prices. (Getty Images: Michael M. Santiago)

## In short:

New York Mayor Zohran Mamdani has announced five free World Cup fan zones will be set up to help counter mounting costs for fans.

"These events were not initially set to be free, but the world's game should belong to the world," Mamdani said.

## What's next?

The World Cup gets underway on June 11 across the United States, Mexico and Canada, with the final set for New York on July 19.

New York City will host free events for soccer fans who can't afford the hefty ticket prices for the FIFA World Cup, Mayor Zohran Mamdani has announced.

The events — one in each of the city's five boroughs — will include watch parties for the matches and other festivities.

They will be staged at Rockefeller Center in Manhattan, the Billie Jean King National Tennis Center in Queens, Brooklyn Bridge Park in Brooklyn, a shopping centre near Yankee Stadium in the Bronx and a minor league baseball stadium in Staten Island.

"These events were not initially set to be free, but the world's game should belong to the world," Mamdani said.

"We've made the decision together for fans to be able to watch that together without having to spend a dollar."

A separate fan event planned for Sports Illustrated Stadium in Harrison, New Jersey, where the New York Red Bulls play, will cost $10.

World Cup matches will be played at MetLife Stadium in New Jersey, [where just getting to and from the matches on public transit could cost $150](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-18/fifa-world-cup-fans-200-train-tickets-for-15-minute-trip-metlife/106579424).

Mamdani, a noted soccer devotee, announced the free events alongside New York Governor Kathy Hochul, a fellow Democrat.

Mamdani framed the initiative as part of a broader effort to ensure local fans are not priced out of the tournament, [amid mounting criticism from fan groups and politicians who warn that rising match ticket costs could exclude ordinary supporters](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2026-04-15/fifa-world-cup-ticket-prices-soar-despite-backlash/106565834).

"Soccer is a game that was born out of the working class," he said.

"The ticket prices that we are seeing are not ones that many working people could even dream of affording.

> "Every fan should be able to watch the greatest tournament on Earth without dipping into their savings."

Officials said fan zones would include live match screenings, food vendors and cultural programs, with more fan activities and community events to be announced in the coming weeks.

Asked about security, Mamdani said preparations were being coordinated with police and other agencies, acknowledging heightened concerns following recent political violence in the country.

"These are events where New Yorkers can celebrate without fear," he said.

"We are confident in our ability to keep people safe."

The New York-New Jersey region will host eight matches, including the final on July 19. The tournament kicks off on June 11 across the United States, Mexico and Canada.

**AP/Reuters**
